speaker: interviewer
text: Today is August 13, 1996. This is an interview with Irene Boris. My name is Clifford Davids. And this is tape number 3. Mrs. Boris.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Last we left off you had arrived at the cotton plantation. Approximately how many other Jewish refugee family or Jewish refugees were there working with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were probably about 10, on this plantation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your accommodations there.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just, we had one room for everybody. They didn't give us nothing, except when you went to work, you had a-- a soup. This soup consisted of flour and water. And that's it. And the rest, you were on your own. Sometimes they gave you a piece bread, and sometimes they didn't have any.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the little things we had, like my mother had a-- a golden watch with all the things, you know, the European people had. So we sold it there to buy a piece of bread. But then even this you-- you didn't. So, like I said, my mother was a very capable woman, and she used to go. And for a piece of wood, she used to bridges-- you know, it was little bridges from the canals in the night, she used to tear them and bring in a little bit of wood to the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they used to take the soup, and bring it home, and put in more water. It was two-- you know, two plates instead of one.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say, everyone lived in one room. Was that all of the refugees who were living there, or just your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ones who were on this plantation, yeah, we were in one room.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many hours would you work every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: It depends-- you have to go up to-- to make the normal. That means you have to give them so much, like you've been with a big apron. And you pulled the cotton. And the more you did, the better. And you had foremans, they used to call them, you know, people who are watching you. And you just couldn't fool around, the sun was-- it's a subtropical climate, very hot there, and over there it's hot really.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you try to wake up early. And they used to wake up early. And then in the daytime, you had about two or three hours off, when the sun started being at the peak you stopped working. And in the summer, it wasn't so bad then. But the winters was even more. You had nothing to-- it was cold.

speaker: interviewee
text: You had no food, no soap, nothing. The dirt, I mean I would say that, you know, all the-- the dirt ate you up alive. And that's how you developed scarlet fever-- not scarlet fever-- let's say the-- malaria everybody had. It was awful, I mean. And then other sicknesses, you know. [NON-ENGLISH] with that sickness skin, get it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see a lot of death?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much. My mother took me to the hospital one day. And I went in, it was maybe 40 beds was in one big room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when this was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't remember the date. I was there in-- she left me for the night. It was-- it was full of people. Next to me was a dead person. They didn't take him out. Because they really didn't have time. And they didn't bother. I mean it was for them the least-- the living one-- they had to Germans were still coming in the priority ward, the-- the army. But still they took us in. They took me in. And in the morning, this one died. This one, they took her out from the room. And we just were like, you know, I mean, didn't bother you. And most of the people from-- from Leningrad, Moscow came there. And it wasn't that they had the priority, but they had better service. Because their men were in the army. You know, big shots. And in this room, 95% died out. And I-- I survived. I mean, we saw a girl, who was talking to us, from typhus-- and fine. In five minutes she was dead. And you took that so casually that you didn't even shed a tear. That's the way it was. People weren't important at the time. A piece of bread was more important than a human being. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] [SOBBING] That's the way it was. I mean, nobody thought about-- you know, people were so-- human flesh was cheap, very cheap. So they didn't make any sense to watch over you. Like if you were sick, you were sick. If you survived, you survived. And that's it. They didn't have too many doctors. And just, when you were in the hospital you had a little bit, you were cleaner. You had water to wash up. And you had a piece of bread every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of clothing were you wearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Clothing wasn't important, either. Whatever you could make it. Shoes, my mother used to make from cotton. She used to make the thread by herself. You make up a little bit of, you know, knit out a pair of slippers, whatever. But you didn't wear them. Because this was the-- you know, the pair that you wore when you went to the city. I mean your-- your feet were so used to. It wasn't-- you know, life wasn't important at the time. It wasn't important. People are dead every single minute. You saw them. You talked to them. In the next minute, they were dead. And like I said, life wasn't important. It's just you wanted to survive. That's it. Just surviving was on your mind. It was, like I say, in '42, '43 till '44, life started being a little bit better.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you at the cotton plantation for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess I was still there till '44-- till middle--

speaker: interviewer
text: From late 1941--

speaker: interviewee
text: '42, '43, I think till the middle of '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family together the whole time?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the plantation, just my-- just my mother and I. My brother they took away to the-- to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did Szloma leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess it was in '42, or maybe '43, I don't remember. But he was a strong, tall guy, six foot tall, maybe more. And they took him away to work. They said, you know, he was older than I was. That-- and a few times, he came, you know, like they gave him a day or two. He came and he brought us some bread, whatever he could save up. And that's all. He didn't know himself. I mean he didn't tell us where he is, what he's doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the-- the approximate three years that you were there, from '41 till '44, did you hear about what was going on back East--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We did.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry, back in Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we did, a little. Not-- we didn't know about the concentration camps. We knew that they're killing Jews. And that thing is that the day they had radios outside on the streets. But they didn't have, you know, in the villages. [BACKROUND NOISE] And I was in the city. And I came up, and I hear that they say in Russian, our Army took over Kostopol, the city of Kostopol. I was so excited. I came home. I run home, and told my mother and so. But, you know, nobody paid attention. Because it was so bad. That you really looked for a piece of bread or a little bit of soup, then politics weren't the main talk. Nobody talked about politics. I mean, your mind wasn't on this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you very isolated at the cotton plantation?

speaker: interviewee
text: You had nobody to talk to there. Because the people who were there, [? were ?] [? Uzbek, ?] you know, the natives. Their lives were different. I'm not going to say they were bad. But, you know, and sometimes they gave you something to eat. You know, sometime they brought up a piece of bread to eat. Sometime they didn't. Because they didn't have too much for themself. And probably they knew about the Germans more than we knew, than we wanted to know. Not that we didn't know, we didn't want to know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you talk with them about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Politics wasn't part of-- of Russian people. They were so used to politics. Nobody-- nobody mentioned any politics.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any number of refugees, Jewish refugees coming, and either staying at the plantation, or passing through, that you had spoken with?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It wasn't-- really, a lot of them who had better situated, like they had a man in the family, and so on and so. Maybe they were from more capable. They tried to avoid, and go to the city, not to stay on the plantation. But I stayed till-- till I had the opportunity, a friend of mine, she was working on the-- it was a factory where are made preserves in-- for the army. And I had a coat. I don't know. It was given to me. And she brought it over there, and they took me in there to work. So I came in there. Then I took my mother in there. And being over there, we had a little bit to eat, fresh food. You know, we could, and whatever we could steal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in '45 or the end of '44, the end of '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went over first, and your mother followed you later? So she stayed at the plantation for a period of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, not too long, not too long. And then we went into the city, and we still lived together with the other two girls who were on the plantation before.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your health like at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: My health was pretty good, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, after she came up from the gangrenous sore, so soon we came to the city, and we start having a little bit of food. Because being on the plantation, my mother used to go in the winter and pick up like garlic, used to bloom in the-- in the winter. So she, you know, that's the greens. And there nothing to steal there even. If you wanted to steal, you couldn't. There was nothing to steal. [BACKROUND NOISE] So you had to live like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: What city was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the-- I mean the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where the factory was located.

speaker: interviewee
text: The cotton plantation?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, where the factory was located.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the factory in Namagan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: N-A-M-A-G-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it wasn't far from the cotton plantation?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was walking about an hour. But over there, it was a lanceman, somebody who was from the same city, and he was there like a watchman. So natural, we could take out something and sell it. And it started being, life was a little better as far as food was concerned. We suffered, but not as bad as on the plantation. It was just, like I said, everybody to me like 90% people died of starvation. But later on in the city, we had already a little bit of food. We had wood. But everything you had to steal. Nobody gave you on a platter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live while you were in the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had-- we went into-- to the-- the girl who took us in there, she was before on the plantation. She left earlier. She took us into her room. We stayed together all the time. I mean, it wasn't-- it was one big-- they had a table. And we slept on the table and under the table. You know, it wasn't-- it was like a dog, dirt and everything. I mean, who cared? It's like you never had a home. And that's-- that's what it was. From over there, like I said, at least we weren't starving.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you live there for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till '46, beginning of '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: So approximately two years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not, not, not exactly, about a year and a half. And then they came up. And it was-- and they said who registered to be from Poland can leave, you know. So I didn't know anything. My mother was more, she was brave. She says, what can we lose? And she registered that we're from Poland, which we were. It wasn't anything. And it start coming from the US, I guess, packages, you know. So they gave us a little bit of-- and they gave me a coat.

speaker: interviewee
text: This coat, and I remember like now, saved my life. Because that's what I gave in Namagan to the girl who was sitting in the office. And she took in me for work. Because I gave her my coat. It was a winter coat. And they said, who wants to go? So naturally, we all walked in. And they said, just by the day, like tomorrow or so, you have to ready near the railroad. We had no luggage, nothing. I mean-- and we just went in. And they brought us into Poland. Let me backtrack a little bit. During the almost two years that you were living in Namagan, you must have been able to have better access to communications. What did you hear about what was going on at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: You had no communications. Russia wasn't a country who gave you what's going on. We knew that the Russian-- on the radio that the Russian Army is doing-- stopped in Leningrad, in like Moscow. But they never gave you the bad part. They just gave you the good things. And especially when you were in this-- you know, in the villages you didn't know anything. There was no radio, nothing. But when you came to the city, in the population it was more-- it was back in the day like Muslim country. And Russia wasn't too fond of them, I mean and be loyal. So you didn't know anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were not aware of what had happened over the last-- almost five years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody knew a little bit, you know, that that's what it is. But nobody knew. That the only thing they knew is some say, oh, I'll come. Probably I'll meet my wife. Because there were a few men there and that of villages. I'll meet maybe my mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: So nobody really realized. Because my grandmother left there my sister. Nobody really knew that coming back to Kostopol, you'll find nobody. I mean, everybody had a-- a vision that probably they lived here, or they lived there, you know. But as time went by, you realized that nobody is there. Had you had any kind of correspondence with either Szloma or Abraham?

speaker: interviewee
text: No absolutely, no. With Abraham, no, no, no, with Szloma either, no correspondence at all, just my mother and I. That's all we were and not with other one.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your mother signed you up to go back to Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So when we-- they had the same trains, you know, the-- like cattle wagon. And they took us to Poland, to Zarow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Z-A-R-O-W.

speaker: interviewer
text: And approximately where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's in Silesia. That was the-- the German-- that the Germans had before. So they took us there. And over there--

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately when was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in the beginning of '46. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, after the war. And then we came there, a lot of refuges from Russia, a lot of them came in. And the first encounter I had standing near the train, a Polish guy came in, and started talking to me in Polish. And he says, he's from the same-- you know like here Massachusetts. You know, [? walling ?] they used to call it. And he says, oh, look at how many they still left. I thought they killed them all. This was my first encounter with anti-Semitism coming back after the war. And he really meant it. Then they took us in. I don't know who. I mean and they gave us-- they gave us-- it was two rooms with another two girls. And this was before occupied, but it was German property that they gave us. And they took us to work there again on the-- you know, on a farm. But my brother looked for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Abraham.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was back with you at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: He came to look for us. I didn't know. And he was-- but they told him that some came into Zarow. So he came, and he was looking for us. And I, by the time, went to look for Jewish people in some other cities. I don't remember even now. And I went on a train. I went first on a street car. And I thought that's what it is, the first stop. Then I had no money.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went again. And I don't know. I went on a train. This time money wasn't so-- being in Poland. And this was the Jewish organizations came to-- to help. What was their name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know I really don't know. It was the Jewish organizations. And that came up, and they said there was a lot of Jews concentrated there. And when I came there, they took us in. And they said-- you know, they ask your name, and this and this. And they says, yeah. There was one of your brothers here. And-- because he was registered there and everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then they said, don't go. Stay here, because this is the-- like a transit place. And he'll come back. So when he find out my mother and whatever they packed, you know, and they came there. So this was in Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't a nice-- and I didn't want to stay either. I was-- I was running. I didn't know from what. But I was running. I didn't stay in Poland. I didn't want their goods. I didn't want nothing, absolutely nothing. We had a nice, you know, apartment, and maybe I could do there on business whatever, but just the first encounter I had was look at how many are still left. I thought they killed them all. And I guess it affected me more than anything. So from over there, they took us by cattle cars to Berlin. We stayed there a few weeks. They--

speaker: interviewer
text: You stayed in Poland a few weeks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in the-- it was like a transit, you know, from the Jewish organizations.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at this point, you-- Abraham had found you, and the-- and the three of you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he came-- a few-- a few days or two weeks or so he came. The other one we don't know till now where he is.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Szloma you're not aware of. So it's your mother, yourself, and Abraham who got on the train and went to Berlin, after a few weeks in the transit camp in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And in Berlin, of course, you know the Russian army got us in there. But the-- the Jewish organizations were in touch with them. And I guess they paid, whatever. Because we were on a cattle car. And they-- they said they are transporting cattles to Berlin. Well, they let us go. And then somebody came in and says no, but you don't understand Russian, Polish, German, nothing. You're deaf and dumb. And we'll do the talking. And they bought us to Berlin. In Berlin, they put us again army barracks, like 20 in a room, 30 or whatever. I don't know. And we stayed there three months. And the-- this was the French zone, we stayed there. And then they came up, and they said you have to go to Germany to the American zone. We came to Munich. In Munich, they kept us, again, maybe a thousand, a few thousand people in one big army room. And everything is history. I don't know how-- who they picked, how they picked. But just, you now, they said after there you have to go to Ulm. And they took us, and they put us again in cars, and they brought us to Ulm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me backtrack a little bit. Was that-- when-- for that short period of time that you were in Poland in the transit camp, did you experience any other type of any anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I just told you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other type of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Other than that one statement?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very much. Wherever you went, you felt it. You felt tense. And I was very sensitive to it, very sensitive. And I just-- like I was the first one to leave. I says, I'm not staying here one minute. I didn't care. But some did. They work, sold, whatever they made, had dollars. I just didn't care.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother feel the same way?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother-- my mother went because I went. I mean she was-- she just was afraid for me, to lose me. And I was just going, not knowing where, but I was going.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from your story, the-- the-- the years that you spent in Russia, you did not experience a lot of anti-Semitism while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the war. And you really didn't had contact with people too much. You didn't. I mean, you didn't go out in a place. You were occupied to find a piece of bread, or a little bit of soup or something else, or a dead cockroach even to eat. You know? You-- I mean, it was on the last agenda, politic wasn't in your-- in your mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about Berlin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Berlin, like I said, we stayed for three months, and we were in army barracks, in the-- what's-- not the UNRRA-- or the HIAS supplied us with food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that a Jewish organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That's the Jewish HIAS. I guess it's the HIAS.

speaker: interviewer
text: HIAS

speaker: interviewee
text: And the UNRRA I think. So they supplied us with food, a kitchen. You went there, and you had some food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to travel around the city at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, you did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were very friendly by that time. You know, they were afraid to open their mouth. They were afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- who's they? The Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans. Very much. They were really afraid. You could kill a German, and nobody will even tell you why. It was-- it was a time that they-- they just felt afraid. They were hiding like mice.

speaker: interviewee
text: So like I says, in Berlin, it was a period. And then they told you, I don't know how they picked, what they picked. They just-- you have to go to Munich. And we went to Munich. And from Munich, we were-- I don't remember how long we were again in a-- in one big room. It was army barracks. And they-- you had to go to the kitchen for food every morning. And then they send us to Ulm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And over there was was sending us that was army barracks too. So we went to live there. And over there it was already a piece of cake.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the three of you were still together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two-- Yeah, my brother Abie, and my mother and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at this point, had you heard anything about Szloma?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely no. I wrote to a lot of the people. I mean, I wrote to the people in Russia there that I knew. I don't remember even now. And no answer.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the rest of your family that you left behind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Nobody knew anything, and from my city, just one girl who survived. I met her here in the United States. But the way I feel, she was-- she was with [NON-ENGLISH]. She said she survived. She was hiding in the cemeteries and this. But we had harsh winters. And so-- so I don't know how she survived. It was the only one. And she was-- really wasn't born in our city. She just got married. So nobody, but nobody. But after the war, I encountered a few men or whatever, and everybody had a little bit to say, when it happened, what had happened. But I have a video now in our house. It was the ghetto. But the ghetto didn't stay too long. It just-- in two or three weeks they liquidated everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're talking about Kostopol?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and it was a place that we used to go there in the summer to [NON-ENGLISH], and they played tennis there. It was-- and over there they had to dig their graves, in-- in the whole city, like in-- in two days. They told them, you going in to the man, whatever. And that's what it was. It says it was one German, and the rest were Ukrainian, a few. It was easy to escape, but nobody dare escape. They were afraid. So everybody was killed there. The ones who were left, were killed there mostly.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's all I remember. I have now the-- video from Kostopol. And I have from my street. Everything is town down. And the only thing I have to say in the video that the day, the week my kids were born, the twins, I had a-- a dream-- dream of my grandmother. And she says, I'm not buried yet, and I'm dead for so long. And I'm here on the corner. Why don't you bury me?

speaker: interviewee
text: I woke up, and I start crying. And here I was pregnant with the twins. I was so big. I went to the rabbi. And he says, you're gonna have a girl. And name her after me, after, you know, Jachna. And that's why my Janet is named after Jachna. I mean, she just said, I'm not buried yet. I'm laying down just like this. So probably her bones were someplace scattered, and nobody knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. We're gonna change tapes now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
